title: What to Do with Old Glasses: 8 Smart Options Beyond the Junk Drawer
description: Old glasses piling up? Here are 8 practical ways to donate, recycle, repurpose, or trade in your old eyeglasses — including options in Edmonton, Alberta.
tldr: Old glasses can be donated to charities like the Lions Club, recycled at optical stores, repurposed as backup pairs, or used for creative DIY projects. Don't just toss them in the trash.

tldrStats.label: People worldwide need glasses but can't afford them
Cost to donate at most optical stores

definition.text: Old glasses are previously worn prescription or non-prescription eyeglasses that are no longer in active use, typically because the wearer's prescription has changed, the frames are damaged, or they've been replaced by a newer pair. These glasses can be donated, recycled, repurposed, or responsibly disposed of rather than discarded.

sections.h2: Why You Shouldn't Just Throw Away Old Glasses
sections.content:
Most of us have at least one or two old pairs sitting in a drawer somewhere. Maybe the prescription is outdated, or the frames went out of style three years ago. Whatever the reason, tossing them in the garbage is a missed opportunity.

Eyeglasses contain materials like metal, plastic, and glass that don't break down easily in landfills. More importantly, your old prescription might be exactly what someone else needs. The World Health Organization estimates that over 2.5 billion people globally need corrective lenses but don't have access to them.

Here are the best ways to give your old glasses a second life.

This is the most impactful option. Organizations like the Lions Club International collect used eyeglasses, clean and sort them by prescription, and distribute them to people in developing countries who can't afford eye care.

In Canada, you can drop off old glasses at many optical stores, community centres, and Lions Club collection boxes. If you're in Edmonton, stores like Charm Optical can point you toward local donation programs.

Other organizations that accept used glasses include Unite For Sight, Optometry Giving Sight, and various local charities across Alberta.

If your glasses are too damaged to donate, recycling is the next best option. Metal frames (titanium, stainless steel, aluminum) are easily recyclable through scrap metal programs. Plastic frames like acetate are trickier but some specialty recyclers accept them.

Lenses made from polycarbonate or CR-39 plastic can sometimes be recycled with other plastics, though this varies by municipality. Check with your local recycling program in Edmonton or Alberta to see what they accept.

Even if your prescription has changed slightly, an old pair can be a lifesaver in emergencies. Keep one in your car, your office desk, or your travel bag. A slightly outdated prescription is far better than no glasses at all when your primary pair breaks or goes missing.

If you're not sure whether your old prescription is still close enough to be useful, bring them to your next eye exam and ask your optometrist. They can tell you how much your vision has changed.

If the frames are still in good shape, many optical shops can pop out the old lenses and fit them with tinted or polarized prescription lenses. This is a cost-effective way to get a second pair of sunglasses without buying entirely new frames.

Ask about lens replacement options at your local optical store. At Charm Optical in South Edmonton, the team can advise whether your old frames are compatible with new lenses.

sections.h2: 5. Donate to a Local School or Community Program
sections.content:
Some schools, shelters, and community health programs in Edmonton and across Alberta maintain small inventories of donated glasses for families who can't afford them. Contact your local school board or community league to see if they have a program in place.

This is especially meaningful for children's glasses, since kids outgrow their prescriptions quickly and many families struggle with the cost of frequent replacements.

sections.h2: 6. Use Them for Crafts and DIY Projects
sections.content:
Old glasses make surprisingly good material for art projects. Remove the lenses and use the frames for costume jewelry, steampunk decorations, or quirky photo booth props. The lenses themselves can be used in small magnifying projects or mosaic art.

Teachers and daycare workers are often happy to take old glasses off your hands for classroom art supplies.

If your old glasses happen to be vintage or designer frames in good condition, they might be worth something. Brands like Ray-Ban, Persol, and certain vintage American Optical frames have active collector markets on eBay, Etsy, and Facebook Marketplace.

Even if they're not worth a fortune, you might recoup enough to put toward your next pair. If you're shopping for new frames, browse what's available at Charm Optical — they carry brands like Ray-Ban, Gucci, Oakley, and more.

sections.h2: 8. Ask Your Optician About Trade-In or Recycling Programs
sections.content:
Some optical stores run their own recycling or trade-in programs. It never hurts to ask when you're picking up your new glasses. Many opticians are happy to take your old pair and make sure it ends up somewhere useful rather than in a landfill.

Next time you visit for an eye exam in Edmonton, bring along any old pairs you want to get rid of. Your optician can help you figure out the best option.

sections.h2: What NOT to Do with Old Glasses
sections.content:
A few things to avoid:

  • Don't give them to someone with a different prescription. Wearing the wrong prescription can cause headaches, eye strain, and even worsen vision over time.
  • Don't throw them in regular recycling without checking your local rules first. Mixed materials (metal hinges, plastic frames, coated lenses) can contaminate recycling streams.
  • Don't keep hoarding them. If you have more than two backup pairs, it's time to donate the rest.

sections.h2: When to Visit Your Optician for Professional Help
sections.content:
While many glasses issues can be handled at home, some problems require professional tools and expertise. Bent metal frames need careful adjustment with pliers designed specifically for eyewear — using household tools risks snapping the frame. Nose pad replacement requires the exact size and shape for your frame model. And any adjustment that changes how the lenses sit in front of your eyes can affect your vision quality.

Most optical stores offer free adjustments for glasses purchased from them. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians will adjust your frames, replace nose pads, tighten screws, and check your lens alignment at no charge — even if your glasses weren't purchased there. This kind of ongoing service is one of the biggest advantages of buying from a local optical store versus ordering online.

If your lenses are scratched, foggy, or the coatings are peeling, it's time for new lenses rather than a repair. Many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical at Charm Optical, and their direct insurance billing means you can use your benefits toward the replacement. book an eye exam online to get your prescription checked while you're there — it may have changed since your last visit.

faqs.q: Can I donate glasses that are scratched or damaged?
faqs.a: Yes, many donation programs accept damaged glasses. Organizations like the Lions Club sort and refurbish donated pairs. Even if the frames are bent, the lenses might still be usable for someone. If they're truly beyond repair, recycling is the better option.

faqs.q: Where can I donate old glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Lions Club collection boxes are found at many Edmonton locations including community centres and some optical stores. You can also check with Charm Optical on Ellerslie Road — they can direct you to the nearest donation drop-off point.

faqs.q: Is it safe to wear someone else's old glasses?
faqs.a: Only if the prescription matches your own. Wearing the wrong prescription can cause headaches, nausea, and eye strain. If you find old glasses and want to use them, have an optometrist check whether the prescription is compatible with your eyes.

faqs.q: Can old lenses be reused in new frames?
faqs.a: Sometimes. If the lenses are in good condition and the shape is compatible with the new frames, an optician can often recut them to fit. This works best when switching between similarly shaped frames.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my glasses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every one to two years. The Canadian Association of Optometrists suggests annual eye exams for adults, and your optometrist will tell you if your prescription has changed enough to warrant new lenses. Book your next eye exam at Charm Optical in South Edmonton to stay on track.
